text: The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. (Sample 1 of the training corpus.)
Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that focuses on data-driven algorithms. (Sample 2 of the training corpus.)
Neural networks are composed of layers of interconnected nodes that process information. (Sample 3 of the training corpus.)
Deep learning has revolutionized computer vision, natural language processing, and speech recognition. (Sample 4 of the training corpus.)
Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to minimize the loss function. (Sample 5 of the training corpus.)
Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight. (Sample 6 of the training corpus.)
Transformers use self-attention mechanisms to process sequential data in parallel. (Sample 7 of the training corpus.)
Convolutional neural networks excel at recognizing spatial patterns in images. (Sample 8 of the training corpus.)
Recurrent neural networks maintain hidden states to process sequences of variable length. (Sample 9 of the training corpus.)
Transfer learning allows models to leverage knowledge from one task to improve another. (Sample 10 of the training corpus.)
Batch normalization stabilizes training by normalizing layer inputs across mini-batches. (Sample 11 of the training corpus.)
Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training to prevent overfitting. (Sample 12 of the training corpus.)
The softmax function converts raw logits into probability distributions over classes. (Sample 13 of the training corpus.)
Cross-entropy loss measures the difference between predicted and true probability distributions. (Sample 14 of the training corpus.)
Adam optimizer combines momentum and adaptive learning rates for efficient training. (Sample 15 of the training corpus.)
Learning rate scheduling adjusts the step size during training for better convergence. (Sample 16 of the training corpus.)
Data augmentation increases training data diversity through transformations like rotation and flipping. (Sample 17 of the training corpus.)
Regularization techniques like L1 and L2 penalties constrain model complexity. (Sample 18 of the training corpus.)
Attention mechanisms allow models to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence. (Sample 19 of the training corpus.)
Positional encodings provide sequence order information to transformer architectures. (Sample 20 of the training corpus.)
The embedding layer maps discrete tokens to dense continuous vector representations. (Sample 21 of the training corpus.)
Tokenization breaks text into smaller units like words, subwords, or characters. (Sample 22 of the training corpus.)
Vocabulary size affects both model capacity and computational requirements. (Sample 23 of the training corpus.)
Multi-head attention allows the model to attend to information from different subspaces. (Sample 24 of the training corpus.)
Layer normalization is applied before each sub-layer in modern transformer architectures. (Sample 25 of the training corpus.)
Feed-forward networks in transformers typically use GeLU or ReLU activation functions. (Sample 26 of the training corpus.)
The residual connections in transformers help gradient flow through deep networks. (Sample 27 of the training corpus.)
Causal masking ensures autoregressive models only attend to previous positions. (Sample 28 of the training corpus.)
Temperature scaling controls the sharpness of the output probability distribution. (Sample 29 of the training corpus.)
Top-k sampling restricts generation to the k most likely next tokens. (Sample 30 of the training corpus.)
Nucleus sampling selects from tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold. (Sample 31 of the training corpus.)
Beam search explores multiple candidate sequences to find high-probability outputs. (Sample 32 of the training corpus.)
Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts a sample of text. (Sample 33 of the training corpus.)
BLEU score evaluates machine translation quality by comparing n-gram overlaps. (Sample 34 of the training corpus.)
The training loop consists of forward pass, loss computation, backward pass, and parameter update. (Sample 35 of the training corpus.)
Mixed precision training uses both 16-bit and 32-bit floating point for efficiency. (Sample 36 of the training corpus.)
Gradient clipping prevents exploding gradients by capping the gradient norm. (Sample 37 of the training corpus.)
Weight initialization strategies like Xavier and He help models converge faster. (Sample 38 of the training corpus.)
The bias-variance tradeoff describes the balance between underfitting and overfitting. (Sample 39 of the training corpus.)
Ensemble methods combine multiple models to improve prediction accuracy and robustness. (Sample 40 of the training corpus.)
